1. Session 4 summary: Day 4. The interrogation had been set for the morning, and so the party went their separate ways the evening before. Samsons, ever the elf who valued his comforts, excused himself from the bastion. His companions stayed behind, the fortress offering beds and a warm meal to those without lodgings in Caer Hollow. Samsons, however, returned to his dormitory at the School of Magic—where a soft mattress and familiar surroundings awaited him. That night, dinner was served within the bastion: a simple but hearty Dutch meal of potatoes, vegetables, and meat, better than the thin stews of the night before. For Samsons, his feast was quite different. He reclined half-dressed in his quarters, a potato sack draped lazily about his waist as he paraded with a goblet of wine in hand. The elf held court among his fellow students, speaking with a smug air. “Ah, you should have seen it,” Samsons boasted, his voice smooth as silk. “The slums reek of rot and desperation, but who else among you can claim to have danced with death in those alleys? I, Samsons of the School, have not only studied theory but lived it! And the prize of my venture? A most peculiar sausage, rotten to its very core, yet fierce enough to nearly choke a man into submission.” His grin widened as his friends laughed, half-believing, half-amused by his dramatics.
    
2. [](https://cdn.discordapp.com/attachments/1393441792354943026/1410929953742127154/samsons_sausage_party.png?ex=69a956ea&is=69a8056a&hm=dd15b15b0d274e1c9d930b68e2466006b65f4907065376e127aec50b3aaa562d&)
    
    ![Image](https://media.discordapp.net/attachments/1393441792354943026/1410929953742127154/samsons_sausage_party.png?ex=69a956ea&is=69a8056a&hm=dd15b15b0d274e1c9d930b68e2466006b65f4907065376e127aec50b3aaa562d&=&format=webp&quality=lossless&width=270&height=350)![](data:image/png;base64,)
    
3. The night passed. At dawn, the party gathered once more in the bastion’s stone halls. Bread and cheese were offered for breakfast, and soon they were led into the chamber of inquiry—the same room where they themselves had been questioned only a day before. The air was cold, heavy with the echo of iron chains. Jaspin sat at the table, his wrists bound in manacles. His hair hung loose around his face, and his eyes darted nervously from the party to the Captain standing behind him. “Before we begin,” Captain Malma said, folding his arms, “I want to hear from you.” His stern gaze fell upon the adventurers. “What exactly did you do to this man? He has lodged complaints—claims of harsh treatment, mumbling something about a sausage choking him.” Samsons smirked, his pride still glowing from the night before, but it was Merios who first answered. “We believed Jaspin too dangerous to allow even a whisper. If he knew magic, he might have slipped our grasp with but a word.” Ivor leaned forward, his voice calm and deliberate. “He had already proven his recklessness by releasing a basilisk in the market. We could take no chances.” At this, Jaspin snapped, rattling his cuffs. “I know no magic! The lock—someone else broke it with sorcery! I had nothing to do with that!” The party pressed him further, their questions sharp as blades. Why the market? Why the basilisk? Who had hired him? Jaspin’s answers were halting, often evasive, until Samsons and Ivor brought forth the ledger and journal they had uncovered the day before. The elf laid the papers upon the table, his slender finger tapping the damning notes: Do not trust the boy with the scar. Small crates dusk watch routes. Two cages, one lizard, on the way to HR, paid in full. Samsons’ voice cut through the room. “Tell us, Jaspin—who is this ‘HR’? Who paid you?”
    
4. The handler bit his lip, silent. Then Merios flicked his hand, conjuring an illusion: a fat, greasy sausage hovering before Jaspin’s face. It swirled mockingly, a phantom reminder of his humiliation. Jaspin paled, his resolve faltering.
    
5. “All right! Enough!” he cried. “I’m no beast-tamer, no grand conjurer. Just a handler, a middle-man! The package wasn’t mine to keep—it was meant for richer hands.” His eyes dropped, shame and fear mingling. “HR… High Ring. The nobles. That’s all I know.” The Captain’s jaw tightened, though he said nothing, letting the party continue. Samsons leaned forward, his tone cutting. “And who else knew of this mission of yours? The broken lock was no accident. Someone wanted this beast free.” Jaspin shook his head. “I told no one, save a man in a black robe. Didn’t even see his face proper. And the basilisk—damn it, I should’ve shielded its eyes. I was a fool.” The adventurers exchanged glances. The High Ring… a noble’s order, perhaps? Someone wealthy enough to afford such a dangerous creature, or reckless enough to unleash it. Merios pressed further. “Who from the High Ring—or the Arcanum—would be angered by the beast’s loss? Who told you to bring it to the market?” “I told you, the market was convenient!” Jaspin barked. “I had other wares to sell, so I stopped there. As for who’s upset over a missing basilisk—I don’t know! I don’t!” The questioning turned to the rat they had found, its body marked and burned. Ivor studied Jaspin with careful eyes, searching for deceit. But Jaspin’s story held: he had found it in the Dusk Watch, and curiosity had kept it with him. No more, no less. The party resolved that their next step would be to consult the High Arcanist, who even now examined the strange creature. When all was said, the judgment was swift. The Captain pronounced Jaspin guilty of bringing a dangerous beast into Caer Hollow. Ten years’ imprisonment. No more, no less. A measure of justice delivered.
    
6. And for their part, the adventurers were rewarded: fifty gold each, a fine cloak commissioned for Merios, tailored to fit her wings and horns with care. It would be ready by the next day, a gift of both practicality and honor. As the party departed the chamber, the shadow of the High Ring lingered in their thoughts. Someone among the city’s elite had dealings with a basilisk. And whoever it was… they remained hidden still.
    
7. ### Fitz![](https://cdn.discordapp.com/clan-badges/504218828968951808/3643e206139d41ad6425aaf3f6d2beaf.png?size=16) _—_ 8/29/2025 6:22 PM
    
    The day was still young when the companions, restless after their interrogation with the Captain, turned their thoughts toward the towering spire of the High Arcanist. Only Samsons, being a student of the arcane, was permitted entry. “Guess we split the party again,” Kyron muttered, folding his arms. But Samsons was clever. With a conspiratorial grin, he gestured to the ever-silent Queek. “This is my emotional support animal,” he said with a confidence that could convince a stone. The guard raised an eyebrow, but Queek, in a rare moment of restraint, remained wordless. To everyone’s surprise, the ruse worked. Inside, Queek’s sharp eyes widened in wonder. The wizard’s tower was a cathedral of magic—walls shimmering with unseen wards, artifacts suspended in glowing cages of energy, and staircases that twisted into impossible angles. For once, the ratling said nothing, only staring in awe. Samsons approached the reception desk where Della sat, quill in hand. Her smile faltered when he presented the sigil of the Crown Watch. “Strange thing for a student to carry,” she murmured. But his insistence and polished tongue wore down her suspicion. With a flick of her wrist, she sent a message through a narrow glass tube, which whisked it away with a faint hiss of magic. “The High Arcanist will reply if he deems it necessary,” she said curtly. Unwilling to linger beneath her watchful eyes, Samsons excused himself and rejoined the others outside. From there, the group wandered to the Arcane Ledge, where an elderly wizard awaited them. Samsons collected the Stoneskin potion he had ordered, though the wizard looked at him gravely. “I tested a drop,” the old man confessed. “Itched for hours. Tasted like gravel. The books say it should not.” “Perhaps you’re allergic,” Samsons said breezily, pocketing the vial. The wizard gave him a look that was equal parts confusion and pity.
    
8. Still, their conversation drifted to the strange rune connected to the dead rat the High Arcanist now studied. “Dwarves may know,” the wizard offered. “Runes are etched into their blood. Or perhaps Miss Alura, Dean of Transmutation and Artifice. She has a keen eye.” A new quest stirred in Samsons’ heart. Before leaving, he inquired about healing potions. The wizard’s expression turned rueful. “None left. The shipments from Dusk Watch have grown unreliable. Lucian was the supplier… I cannot brew without moonshade moss.” Samsons leaned in, smile sharp. “Then if we secure your supplies, you’ll have us as your best customers. Perhaps a discount, hmm?” The wizard sighed, already regretting the bargain. Later, with no clear direction, the friends did what all restless souls do—they sought a tavern. At the bar, Samsons slammed down spiced rum for everyone. Kyron and Merios, unimpressed, declined. “Ungrateful,” Queek grumbled. Queek, however, downed his portion with gusto, warmth spreading through his small body until even his whiskers seemed more confident. Nearby, miners spoke in low tones. Queek, now braver, leaned in. “Which miners?” he asked. “Iron Hold,” one answered. “Dwarves. Striking for better wages. Five days by horse.” Another shook his head. “And Dusk Watch shipments are stalled again. Always trouble at border towns.” The party, mischief in their eyes, began seeding rumors of their own—whispers that the High Ring itself was behind the basilisk attack. A rival within the Ring had released the beast as a distraction to steal something of far greater value. The story spread like smoke. In the middle of this revelry, Samsons’ eyes went wide. “The High Arcanist!” he exclaimed, and with a clumsy stumble, he rushed back to the tower, Queek at his heels. This time, a different guard barred Queek’s entry. “Comfort animal?” the guard scoffed. “Try harder.”
    
9. Samsons entered alone, swaying slightly from the rum. At the reception, Della glanced up and frowned. He leaned heavily on her desk. “I need the message—your message—the Arcanist’s message,” he slurred. “The High Arcanist has not responded,” Della said flatly. Flustered, he blurted, “Then what about Miss Alura? Is she here?” Moments later, the Dean of Transmutation and Artifice appeared, robes trailing, her gaze sharp as polished steel. Samsons tried, in his drunken state, to explain the rune’s danger. “Unknown… might harm him… must be warned…” Miss Alura cut him off. “I have seen the rune. It bears no threat. The High Arcanist has already secluded himself with it. You, young man, would do well to remember this: every scrap of knowledge you claim, you learned within these walls. Do you truly believe the High Arcanist and I would know less than you?” Her disappointment stung worse than any slap. Samsons lowered his eyes, words failing him. By nightfall, the companions gathered at the Bastion. Maps spread before them revealed Dusk Watch in the southwest and Iron Hold to the east. Merios’ home lay along the way. Choices were before them: the unrest of miners, the missing shipments, and whispers of strange runes. And so they rested, knowing that tomorrow, the road would call them once more.
    
10. _ Day 5. Samsons had decided to remain at the bastion that night, choosing the company of his newfound companions over the loneliness of his student dormitory. He feared missing out, and truth be told, there was a certain comfort in the camaraderie of the others. Together they shared a modest breakfast in the hall before the Captain herself arrived, her boots striking hard against the stone floor as she approached. She wasted no time. “Jaspin has been readied,” she said. “At your request, Samsons.” So they climbed. The stairwell spiraled endlessly upward through the bastion’s keep until they reached the chambers where prisoners were kept. The air was close, heavy with damp and iron. Jaspin was dragged forward—shackled, his eyes still darted nervously about the room, probably looking for any sausage nearby. Samsons stepped forward. “The rat,” he asked again, his voice steady. “Tell me what you know.” Jaspin shook his head, the chains rattling as he lifted his hands. “I’ve said it before. I don’t know what it is, or what it does. I found it in the streets of Dusk Watch. Curious thing, a dead rat, burnt rune carved in its hide. Thought I’d take it home, have a look. But no one… no one in Dusk Watch looked at me. That’s what I remember most. It was wrong. As if I wasn’t even there.” The party exchanged uneasy glances. His story hadn’t changed, yet it made little sense. Jaspin wasn’t a mage, nor a scholar of runes. He was an animal handler by trade. And what kind of handler collects dead things? Possibly cursed things? It rang false. At a signal from the Captain, the guards dragged Jaspin back to his cell. She turned to the party, arms crossed over her breastplate. “There are two matters I could use your help with,” she said. “First, the shipments out of Dusk Watch.
    
11. They’ve gone missing. Scouts whisper the place is near a ghost town now. Second, the Iron Hold. The miners there have stopped working—strange noises in the deep, dwarves disappearing. Fear rules the tunnels.” Her gaze hardened. “I’ve an elite squad at my disposal, but I’m short-staffed. They’ll take whichever task you refuse. You, however, will have the first choice. Consider it a reward for bringing Jaspin in so swiftly.” The group conferred quietly. Dusk Watch held the burned rat, the whispers of a ghost town, and the promise of free healing draughts if they restored the shipments. Iron Hold meant dwarves, and Queek’s skin had already gone pale at the mention. The decision was made. “We’ll take Dusk Watch,” Merios said at last. The Captain nodded once. “So be it. A dangerous task, but one worthy of your talents. Return with information, and you’ll be paid one hundred gold each. Solve the problem, and you’ll double it.” Arrangements were made swiftly. Four horses, two harnessed to a stout carriage, with rations and supplies enough for the journey there and back. A small care package from the bastion’s healers was tucked inside as well. Before departure, Merios asked the Captain to send word once more to her parents—that she was leaving Caer Hollow again, bound for dangers she would not detail. She knew the reply would not reach her, but the request gave her heart some measure of ease. And so, with iron-shod hooves striking sparks on the cobblestones, the company rode out in the afternoon. Their path lay toward the southwest, where the ghost town of Dusk Watch waited, and with it, the truth of the burned rune and the silence that gripped its streets.
